summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW - SPOUSAL MAINTENANCE – Where wife seeks interim order for periodic spousal maintenance in context of application pursuant to section 79A of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) to set aside final property orders made in 2015 – Whether wife is unable to support herself adequately – Whether husband reasonably able to support wife

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

PARKER J:

INTRODUCTION

The application before the Court is the application of the wife, MS ERICKSON (“the wife”), for interim spousal maintenance. She seeks periodic payments of $2,200 per week.[1] The husband, MR ERICKSON (“the husband”), seeks that the application be dismissed.

BACKGROUND

The wife was born in 1970 and is aged 55. She is employed in the financial services industry. The husband was born in 1972 and will shortly turn 54. He is employed in the construction industry.
The parties were married in 2002 and commenced cohabitation at that time. There are two children of their marriage, MS B born in 2004, currently aged 21, and X born in 2009 and currently aged 16. Both of the parties’ daughters live with the wife. They are both full-time students and are financially dependent upon the wife.
The parties separated on 13 July 2013 after a period of cohabitation of approximately 11 and a half years.
The parties engaged in property proceedings in what was then the Federal Circuit Court of Australia in 2014 and 2015. Final consent orders were made between the parties and the husband’s parents as interveners on 11 November 2015, and further final consent orders were made between the parties only on 12 November 2015 (“the final orders”).
The wife’s evidence was that the parties subsequently reconciled, recommenced cohabitation in May 2016 and continued their marriage relationship for a further period of approximately seven years before finally separating on or around 27 May 2023.
The husband disputes that the parties reconciled for that period. He acknowledges that they attempted a reconciliation from late 2016 to mid-2018, and that he resumed residence in the former matrimonial home at C Street, Suburb D, South Australia (“the former matrimonial home”) in August 2016 and remained there until 2023. His evidence, however, is that the attempt at reconciliation failed, that the parties lived separated under the one roof from 2018, that they maintained separate finances throughout the time that he resided in the former matrimonial home after the 2013 separation, and that he paid rent, which was $650 per week until late 2018 when it increased to $900 per week.
A divorce order was made in 2024 and became final in late 2024.
The wife commenced the present proceedings on 29 November 2024. She seeks that the final orders be set aside pursuant to section 79A of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). The wife’s application has two bases, as follows:
(a) miscarriage of justice by reason of suppression of evidence (subsection 79A(1)(a)). The wife’s application in this regard is based on allegations that the husband failed to disclose to her and to the Court (and indeed expressly denied) substantial interests held by him in property owned by his late brother and/or liabilities owed to him by his brother prior to the making of the final orders, which interests were shortly thereafter asserted, pursued and recovered by him; and

(b) implied consent to vary or set aside the orders arising from the circumstances of the asserted reconciliation (subsection 79A(1A)).

EVIDENCE AND SUBMISSIONS

The parties were each cross-examined by counsel for the other at the hearing of the spousal maintenance application.
Although each and every aspect of the evidence relied upon by the parties has not been referred to in these reasons, I have had regard to the totality of the evidence before the Court and the submissions of each of the parties.

THE LEGAL FRAMEWORK

Section 74 of the Act provides that the Court may make such order as it considers proper for the maintenance of one of the parties to a marriage. Section 72 provides that in exercising jurisdiction under section 74, the Court must apply the principle that a party to a marriage is liable to maintain the other party, to the extent that the first-mentioned party is reasonably able to do so, if, and only if, that other party is unable to support herself or himself adequately whether:
(a) by reason of having the care and control of a child of the marriage who has not attained the age of 18 years;

(b) by reason of age or physical or mental incapacity for appropriate gainful employment; or

(c) for any other adequate reason.

An award of maintenance requires a threshold finding as to the requisite inability,[2] having regard to the matters in subsection 75(2).[3] The party seeking maintenance bears the onus of satisfying the Court that the threshold requirement is met.
“Adequately” has been interpreted as meaning to mean a standard of living that is reasonable in all the circumstances, including the circumstance of the parties’ separation.[4] There is no fettering principle that the parties’ pre-separation standard of living must automatically be awarded where the respondent’s means permit, and discretion is to be exercised in accordance with section 74, with “reasonable in the circumstances” as the guiding principle.[5] What is “adequate” is not to be determined by reference to any fixed or absolute standard, but by having regard to any relevant matter referred to in subsection 75(2).[6] It will often not be appropriate for one party to enjoy a much higher standard of living than the other.[7]
In Brown & Brown,[8] the Full Court of the Family Court of Australia (as the Court was then known) (“the Full Court”) summarised the applicable principles as follows:
(a) The word “adequately” is not to be determined according to any fixed or absolute standard.

(b) The idea that “adequate” means a subsistence level has been firmly rejected.

(c) Where possible both spouses should continue to live after separation at the level which they previously enjoyed if this is reasonable, although the parties’ standard of living may have to be lower if financial resources are insufficient to maintain that standard.

(d) In some circumstances it may be reasonable for the parties to live at a higher standard than previously enjoyed.

(e) It is not necessary for an applicant for maintenance to use up all capital in order to satisfy the requirement that he/she is unable to support himself/herself adequately.

(f) However, an applicant is not entitled to live at a level of considerable luxury or comfort merely because the other party is very wealthy.

In Saxena & Saxena,[9] Coleman J set out the process to be adopted in in determining whether to make an order for spousal maintenance, as follows:
(1) Can the applicant support themselves adequately?

(2) If not, what are the applicant’s reasonable needs?

(3) What capacity does the respondent have to meet those needs?

(4) What order is reasonable, having regard to subsection 75(2)?

Each party’s necessary financial commitments must be taken into account,[10] but unnecessary or unreasonable expenditure may be disregarded.[11] The earning capacity, and not just the present income, of each of the parties will be relevant.[12] Expenses for children are generally not to be taken into account as part of the assessment of the reasonable needs of the applicant,[13] and correspondingly, child support payments are not to be considered as income available to meet the needs of the applicant for maintenance. Child support obligations must be taken into account.[14] The income and expenses of other members of each party’s household are relevant considerations.[15]
The test of ability to support oneself is whether the applicant is in a position to finance him or herself from his or her own resources. That is to say, the test is whether by reason of earning capacity, capital or other sources of income, the applicant is in a position to look after him or herself.[16]
As to whether the respondent to an application for maintenance is “reasonably able” to support the applicant, regard should be had to the income of the first-mentioned party and then the unavoidable, non-discretionary expenses of that party, including his or her reasonable living expenses. After that exercise, one can consider the amount, if any, from which the first party may be able to contribute to the maintenance of the other party.[17] Regard must be had to the property, financial resources and earning capacity of the respondent.[18] Although maintenance is ideally met from income, property and financial resources can also be applied for this purpose.[19]

INTERIM SPOUSAL MAINTENANCE

The power conferred by section 74 of the Act includes a power to make an order for spousal maintenance pending the disposal of the proceedings or until further order.[20] In an interim application with respect to spousal maintenance, the evidence need not be so extensive or the evidence so precise as on an application for a final order.[21] The Court is entitled to take a broad brush approach in making findings and expressing reasons and can determine the issues in a summary fashion.[22] Nonetheless, an interim order for spousal maintenance requires a finding on the balance of probabilities that the threshold requirement in section 72 is met.[23]
Matters which are relevant in the specific context of an interim application for spousal maintenance include the time that would elapse between the interim hearing and the final hearing and the possibility that any depletion of capital could, provided sufficient assets were available, be restored or otherwise taken into account in the final property orders.[24]
It was submitted in the husband’s case outline that the periodic spousal maintenance order was not “interim” in nature and would in reality operate as a final order, requiring the husband to satisfy one of the criteria in subsection 83(2) of the Act in order to secure its discharge. I do not agree. In my view, an order made “until further order” (such as the order sought by the wife) in the context of extant financial proceedings is by its nature an interim order.[25] As a consequence, any such order will automatically be discharged upon the making of final orders disposing of the proceedings by virtue of the operation of rule 5.01 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth), which provides that “any interlocutory order made in the proceeding pending further order is automatically discharged and ceases to have continuing effect.”
Any order made in the context of the present application will therefore be of limited duration (noting that this matter is presently listed for trial in early September 2026, less than seven months hence). For this reason, I shall have regard to both the wife’s ability to provide for her own support and the husband’s capacity to provide for her support in the context of the immediate future rather than on the basis of long-term considerations.

SUBSECTION 75(2) FACTORS

The factors in subsection 75(2) which are relevant to the present matter are considered in the following paragraphs.

(a) the age and state of health of each of the parties

The parties are of similar ages. There is no evidence of any significant health afflictions suffered by either of them.

(b) the income, property and financial resources of each of the parties and the physical and mental capacity of each of them for appropriate gainful employment

The wife’s evidence was of having net income of $1,822 per week, outgoings averaging $4,039 per week, and therefore a shortfall of $2,217 per week. Of her total disclosed income, $37 per week is in the form of Centrelink family assistance benefits, which, as an income tested benefit, must be disregarded.[26] The sum of $299 per week (which it is agreed has risen to $311 per week since the filing of the wife’s Financial Statement) is in the form of child support received from the husband with respect to X and is therefore not income that is available to the wife for her own self-support. The balance of the wife’s disclosed income is her employment income. It was not suggested that she is not utilising her full capacity for gainful employment.
The wife holds the following major assets:
(a) The former matrimonial home, the husband’s interest in which was transferred to her in February 2016 pursuant to the final orders. She estimates that this property has a value of $1,370,000;

(b) A vacant block of land at E Street, Suburb F, South Australia (“the [Suburb F] block”), the value of which she estimated as $750,000;

(c) A 50 per cent interest in an unencumbered property at G Street, Suburb H, South Australia (“the [Suburb H] property”), which is owned as tenants in common with her brother. This interest was inherited from her late brother, following his death in 2010. She estimated her interest in this property to have a value of $730,000; and

(d) A five per cent interest in a property at J Street, Suburb K, being a property in which her brother and other persons also hold interests. She estimated her interest in this property to be $36,550.

The wife’s Financial Statement disclosed no substantial funds held in bank accounts and no other assets of significance other than superannuation in the sum of $200,474.
The wife’s major liability is a loan owed to L Bank secured by mortgage encumbering both the former matrimonial home and the Suburb F block. The loan has a balance of $721,256 (as at 15 September 2025, being the date of filing of the wife’s Financial Statement). This liability was retained by the wife pursuant to the final orders.
The wife’s Financial Statement also discloses loans owed to her mother in the sum of $55,000 and to “friends & church” in the sum of $10,500, as well as her share of a liability for school fees owed by the parties, being $16,710, and arrears of council rates in the sum of $4,992.
According to the wife’s Financial Statement, she has a net asset position (excluding superannuation) of $2,085,548, made up of $2,889,006 in assets and liabilities totalling $803,458.
The wife’s evidence with respect to the Suburb H property was that her brother and co-owner managed the finances with respect to the property, and that the share of the rent apportioned to her during the 2024/2025 financial year had been $15,600 before tax but that she had not in fact received any of those funds until 22 November 2025, when she had asked her brother to give her $2,000 to pay outstanding bills.
The wife’s evidence with respect to the timeline of tenancies of that property was difficult to follow. She initially deposed that it had been rented “for the first time” during the 2024/2025 financial year. She later deposed that it had been rented by the current tenant, who is a friend of her brother’s, since 2023, though the same paragraph also referred to it having been vacant for a year from April or May of 2023. She otherwise provided a history of the property having been tenanted consistently from 2015 to 2023 (save that the tenant had paid no rent for a period of six months ending some time in 2021). Ultimately, little turns on this issue.
The wife’s evidence was that the current tenant pays rent of $650 per week, being a rate she described as “minimal” and which she accepted was below market rates. She is not compensated for the loss in her own potential to derive rental income arising from this arrangement made with her brother’s friend.
The wife deposed that the rental income had been accumulated by her brother to save for substantial repairs that were required to be done to the Suburb H property. She gave evidence under cross-examination that she when things had become “really bad” she had started to ask her brother for money from that account when she needed it, and he had provided her with two payments of $2,000 and one payment of $1,000 from the rental account, and had made equivalent payments to himself. Her evidence was that the sum of $12,000 remained in the account, of which she was entitled to fifty per cent.
Irrespective of whether the rental income is physically received by her or saved in an account used for future expenditure on the property, it is income received by the wife and both the rental income and the accumulated funds should have been, but were not, disclosed in her Financial Statement.
The husband’s Financial Statement disclosed that he receives $6,003 per week in income and has expenses totalling $6,131 per week, leaving a shortfall of $128 per week.
His disclosed income is made up of $3,258 per week in employment income and $2,745 in rental income.
The husband deposed to being employed by M Company on a contract basis with a fluctuating income of approximately $3,258 per week.
The husband has previously worked as a self-employed trade and has engaged in the construction industry. He operates or has operated a business known as N Company through a corporation by the name of P Pty Ltd. He deposed that this entity was not presently generating any income but proffered no explanation as to the reason for this.
The husband’s Financial Statement recorded rental income of $2,345. The wife alleged, by reference to his bank statements, that this had been understated by $82 per week. Her evidence in this regard was not challenged.
The husband deposed to his parents lending him approximately $150 per week in cash to assist him with expenses such as groceries, bills and home loan repayments on an “as needs” basis.
The expenses disclosed in the husband’s Financial Statement included $913 in income tax, a total of $2,644 in mortgage repayments, $310 per week in child support paid to the wife and $30 per week paid towards car registration and insurance for his partner MS O.
The husband’s Financial Statement recorded a net asset position (excluding superannuation) of $958,515, made up of assets with a total value of $3,035,814 and liabilities of $2,077,299.
The husband’s major disclosed assets are:
(a) a property at Q Street, Suburb R, South Australia (which he asserts is subject to deeds executed in favour of his parents), which for which he gives an estimated value of $850,000;

(b) six units at S Street, Suburb T, South Australia, for which he gives an estimated total value of $2,085,512; and

(c) two motor vehicles with a combined estimated value of $83,000.

He also disclosed superannuation interests with a combined value of $206,800.
The husband’s major disclosed liabilities are:
(a) mortgage liabilities totalling $595,687;

(b) assessed and unpaid income tax totalling $18,000;

(c) a Westpac business loan in the sum of $50,000;

(d) a loan in the sum of $115,000 to his parents, said to relate to a deed;

(e) a loan in the sum of $19,731 owed to his parents for legal fees;

(f) a loan in the sum of $37,000 owed to a person by the name of Mr U for legal fees;

(g) a loan in the sum of $40,000 owed to “[Mr V]” for legal fees;

(h) a debt owed to his self-managed superannuation fund in the sum of $103,235; and

(i) his share of a debt jointly owed by the parties with respect to school fees (in the sum of $19,208).

Although the husband’s Financial Statement also included a liability owed to his lawyer for legal fees, the costs notice filed on his behalf pursuant to rule 12.06 of the Rules ahead of the hearing of the present application indicated that he had no legal fees outstanding.[27]
Counsel for the husband emphasised that on its face, the wife’s net asset position was superior to that of the husband.
The wife alleged that there were serious deficiencies in the husband’s disclosure with respect to his financial circumstances. These alleged deficiencies included failure to disclose payslips after May 2025, his 2025 income tax return, any financial reports for any business entities (or documents demonstrating the wind up thereof), the source of significant cash deposits to his bank accounts and the use of significant cash withdrawals, documents relating to the taxation debt asserted by him, and any documents relating to Ms O’s financial circumstances.
The wife also alleged that there were difficulties and inconsistencies in the available evidence with respect to the husband’s income, including (but not limited to) the following:
(a) For the financial year ending 30 June 2025:
(i) the husband has disclosed payslips from his employer, M Company recording a gross income of approximately $140,000 for the financial year ending 30 June 2025; and

(ii) his provisional income was assessed for child support purposes on 21 August 2025 as $114,486.

(b) For the financial year ending 30 June 2024:

(i) the husband’s taxation Notice of Assessment recorded his taxable income as $65,860;

(ii) on or around 26 May 2024, the husband applied for (and obtained) a loan in the sum of $608,000 after declaring on a “self-employed declaration of financial position” that his taxable income after all business expenses was $237,000; and

(iii) His provisional income for child support purposes was assessed at $243,830 on 24 July 2024, at $120,000 on 6 August 2024, and at $108,312 on 14 March 2025.

(c) The husband’s Financial Statement discloses rental income of $2,345 per week, but the applicable rental statements demonstrate income of approximately $2,427.49 per week (a difference of approximately $82 per week).

The wife was not challenged with respect to these assertions and no documentary evidence was adduced to refute them.
The husband’s costs notice filed on 8 September 2025[28] disclosed payments of legal fees in the total sum of $140,348 during the course of these proceedings, of which $99,913 had been paid since an earlier costs notice had been filed approximately 90 days prior on 10 June 2025. That costs notice disclosed that these costs had been paid from his income.
The first costs notice filed by the husband after the filing of the wife’s affidavit making reference to his earlier costs notices having disclosed the payment of significant legal fees from his income asserted for the first time that his fees had been paid from his own income as well as “as well as loans from [his] parents, friends and [Ms O].” His affidavit filed on 5 November 2025 similarly asserted that he was meeting his legal fees from “[his] personal income, via loans from [my] parents and friends.” He deposed in that affidavit, contradicting his earlier filed costs notices, that he had paid $45,000 in legal fees from his own funds, and admitted that he had paid legal fees of $99,913 over a period of approximately 90 days but asserted that he had not advised his solicitors that he had needed to borrow cash from family and friends to do so. He did not explain why he had told them that the funds had been derived from his income.
The costs notice filed on behalf of the husband prior to the hearing of the present application[29] indicated that his total expenditure on legal fees for the present proceedings had been $203,063.59.
When cross-examined about the contents of his costs notices, the husband gave evidence to the following effect:
(a) the sum of $45,000 had been paid from his own funds (or those of P Pty Ltd);

(b) $47,000 had been paid by his friend Mr V;

(c) $37,000 had been paid by his family friend Mr U;

(d) his parents had paid around $18,000;

(e) the balance had been paid by Ms O, from whom he said he had borrowed approximately $80,000;

(f) all of the funds from third parties had been provided in cash to Ms O, who had then deposited them into her bank account and then paid them to his lawyers; and

(g) his costs notices saying that he had paid his legal fees from income had been incorrect, and he had “mentioned” something to his solicitor but she had not asked him “specifically” whether the money had come out of his accounts, “so that got corrected.”

The husband produced no independent documents such as receipts or signed loan agreements to establish the existence of the claimed loans, nor statements demonstrating the asserted deposits into Ms O’s bank account.
During the course of his cross-examination, the husband asserted that the various payments to which he had referred had been recorded in a ledger. Counsel for the wife called for the ledger and a notebook (“the ledger”) was ultimately produced (after a delay of some hours during which the Court was told that Ms O had been tasked with collecting it upon the husband having forgotten to bring it) the following day.
There were a number of difficulties with the evidence given by the husband with respect to these issues and the ledger produced by him, as follows:
(a) The husband’s evidence of having borrowed the sum of approximately $80,000 from Ms O to fund his legal fees did not sit well with other evidence given by him as to having paid significant expenses on her behalf, including having paid legal fees incurred by her in the sum of $52,679 (which he said was done to “assist”) and the discharge of her car loan in the sum of $67,506 (which he said he had done “because she couldn’t afford it”). When confronted with this suggestion, the husband pivoted and said that part of the funds paid by Ms O towards his legal fees had been repayment of the funds he had applied to her legal fees, and that he owed her “the balance.”

(b) The husband gave evidence that he had purchased Motor Vehicle 1 for the sum of $36,795 for his mother in December 2024 because she “needed to get around” and his parents “didn’t have the financial means” yet asserted that his parents had lent him substantial sums for living expenses and legal fees. He said that they had been able to provide these funds to him because “they had money on the side like every European does.” It was not clear whether his assertion that “every European” has “money on the side” included himself.

(c) He was unable to explain why some of the various cash payments said to have been made by third parties had been made to Ms O rather than to him, and why they had all been paid into her bank account and not his, particularly in light of his evidence that their finances were kept separate.

(d) He was unable to explain why a later date appeared at an earlier point in the ledger than an earlier date.

(e) He was unable to explain why payments received from Mr V had been allocated as loans extended by Mr V to him, rather than as repayments towards a loan he asserted Mr V owed him during the same period, as recorded on a different page of the same ledger.

(f) He was unable to explain the meaning of an annotation in the ledger.

(g) He initially said that he had written the ledger entries but later said that all but the first page had been written by Ms O and that he had not seen the entries made by her prior to the hearing, before saying that he had seen her write them.

(h) A payment of $1,059.49 was somewhat curiously recorded as having been made in cash.

(i) The ledger entries recorded indebtedness similar to but not exactly the same as the debts recorded in the husband’s Financial Statement as at the date of that document and the differences were not explained (though I note the husband was not asked about this).

Following the evidence given by the husband with respect to the contents of the costs notices filed on his behalf, the hearing was adjourned part-heard on his application so that counsel could consider the potential need for the husband’s solicitor to give evidence. When the hearing resumed, a statement of agreed facts was tendered.[30] The agreed facts were as follows:
(a) Prior to 30 September 2025 the [husband] at all times provided instructions to his solicitors, to the effect that payment of legal fees was made from income.

(b) All costs notices required under rule 12.06 were undertaken in accordance with the [husband]’s instructions.

(c) Prior to providing instructions on 30 September 2025, the [husband] read the affidavit of the applicant that was filed on 15 September 2025.

(d) Thereafter the [husband] provided instructions that the source of funding was from income and third party loans.

Counsel for the wife submitted that the husband’s evidence had been inconsistent with the statement of agreed facts. It certainly sat uncomfortably with it.
The husband accepted that he had registered a business for GST in 2024 but denied that this had been with the expectation of earning an income. He asserted that it had been “for refinancing purposes” but acknowledged that the entity in question had no real property holdings. He sought to explain this by saying that it had been “just to get the finance over the line” and “to provide information for the application.” This explanation was difficult to follow.
The husband’s bank statements included payments made to the Australian Taxation Office (“ATO”) in the sums of $18,000 and $10,000 within two weeks in late 2023 and early 2024. The wife alleged that these constituted evidence of ongoing trading. The husband asserted that these had been Business Activity Statement GST payments made on behalf of a third party by the name of Mr W. In the witness box, the husband asserted that Mr W was his friend Mr V’s business partner and that he had made these payments on behalf of Mr W because “he needed funds at the time” and had subsequently been repaid “within a few weeks.”
The ledger recorded repayment of these sums as having occurred between March and October 2024. The husband was unable to explain why one of the purported repayments (a bank deposit of $3,000 on 15 March 2024) bore the annotation “Site Works [Mr V].”[31] He also asserted that a payment of $2,600 received by him on February 2024 bearing the annotation “tenancies bond” was a payment from Mr V, but was unable to explain why it had been labelled as a tenancies bond. That payment did not appear on the ledger.

(c) the extent to which either party has the care of a child of the marriage who has not attained the age of 18 years, including the need of either party to provide appropriate housing for such a child

The wife has the full-time care of the parties’ 16 year old daughter X and has a need to provide appropriate housing for her. X spends no time with the husband and he therefore does not require housing for her.

(d) commitments of each of the parties that are necessary to enable the party to support (i) himself or herself; and (ii) a child or another person that the party has a duty to maintain

The parties each have obligations with respect to support of Ms B notwithstanding the fact that she has attained the age of 18 in circumstances in which she is undertaking tertiary education and remains financially dependent, notwithstanding the absence of an order in this regard.[32] The wife is the only party currently meeting that obligation. However, the appropriate application with respect to meeting the expenses of an adult child is an application for adult child maintenance,[33] not spousal maintenance, and there is no such application before the court. I note further that Ms B works on a part-time basis as a legal professional. According to the wife’s Financial Statement, she earns $600 per week.

(f) subject to subsection (3), the eligibility of either party for a pension, allowance or benefit under (i) any law of the Commonwealth, of a State or Territory or of another country; or (ii) any superannuation fund or scheme, whether the fund or scheme was established, or operates, within or outside Australia and the rate of any such pension, allowance or benefit being paid to either party

Subsection 75(3) provides that in exercising its jurisdiction under section 74, a court shall disregard any entitlement of the party whose maintenance is under consideration to an income tested pension, allowance or benefit. The wife receives family assistance payments from Centrelink, which is a means tested benefit and is therefore to be disregarded.
The husband asserted that the wife also received a parenting pension but admitted under cross-examination that he had no basis for making that assertion other than his awareness that the wife held a Health Care Card.

(g) where the parties have separated or divorced, a standard of living that in all the circumstances is reasonable

It is usual, when considering this factor, to have regard to the standard of living enjoyed by each of the parties to the marriage prior to their separation, including the extent to which the respondent was, prior to the separation, making payments towards expenses for the benefit of the applicant. In the present case, there is an as yet unresolved dispute as to when the parties’ final separation occurred.
The wife’s evidence was that during the parties’ relationship, until what she says was their final separation in 2023, they shared payment of the mortgage and other expenses in circumstances in which her income alone was not sufficient to support the family.
Even on the husband’s evidence, in the period leading up to which the wife asserts was the date of their final separation, he was meeting significant expenses on her behalf, including payments towards the mortgage, other outgoings such as rates and taxes, repairs and improvements to the former matrimonial home, repayment of debts on her behalf, paying for training and travel undertaken by her, and providing other substantial lump sum payments directly to her for her benefit. He also deposed to having paid for private school fees for the parties’ children during that period (which he is no longer doing) and accepted under cross-examination that he had contributed to other family expenses, that there had been a general sharing of finances between the parties, and that any shortfall experienced by the wife had been covered by him.
It is therefore evident that prior to 2023, irrespective of whether the parties were in fact continuing their marriage relationship, the wife enjoyed a standard of living that was significantly higher than that which she can afford to sustain using her own resources alone, and that the husband demonstrated the financial wherewithal to provide such a standard of living over an extended period in addition to meeting (what were throughout that period) his own necessary expenses.
The wife also gave unchallenged evidence that for approximately a month following husband ceasing to reside at the former matrimonial home, he attended at the home and gave her approximately $2,500 in cash each fortnight. She otherwise deposed that the husband transferred the sum of $1,800 into her bank account on 29 June 2023 and the sum of $2,800 on 14 July 2023.

(j) the extent to which the party whose maintenance is under consideration has contributed to the income, earning capacity, property and financial resources of the other party

The evidence before the Court suggests that substantial funds derived from the “economic partnership”[34] of the parties’ marriage were or were likely to have been dedicated to and invested in the husband’s property development endeavours.

(k) the duration of the marriage and the extent to which it has affected the earning capacity of the party whose maintenance is under consideration

The parties were married for over 22 years, and theirs was therefore a marriage of long duration. The extent to which they lived as husband and wife, as opposed to having been separated under one roof, for a number of the years of that marriage is a matter of contention which has not yet been determined. However, it is apparent that the wife has performed the role of primary caregiver to the parties’ children throughout the entirety of that period. Whilst the evidence does not permit specific findings as to the impact of that role on her earning capacity, as a matter of logic, a person with the primary responsibility for the care of children is less free to create and develop career and business opportunities than is a person whose spouse performs the primary caregiving role.[35]

(m) if either party is cohabiting with another person--the financial circumstances relating to the cohabitation

The husband has repartnered and is cohabiting with Ms O. He deposed that he and Ms O have agreed to keep their finances separate and that Ms O pays him rent of $400 per week, which rent the wife alleged was not disclosed in any documents produced by the husband by way of discovery. The wife’s evidence in this regard was not challenged.
The husband further deposed that Ms O receives a carer’s pension of $746 per week for care she provides to the husband’s mother. How she was purportedly in a position to lend substantial money to the husband for payment of his legal fees when reliant on a carer’s pension was not explained.
The wife deposed that Ms O was registered as a sole trader on the Australian Business Register as at 29 October 2025. The husband was unable to explain this, or her registration for GST, other than to say “she just wanted to.” He said there was “nothing wrong with registering a business” but denied that she was operating a business.
The husband’s evidence that he and Ms O maintain separate finances was somewhat contradicted by his evidence that he provides financial support to her, including the reference in his Financial Statement to the payment of $30 per week in motor vehicle-related expenses for her benefit. Part N of his Financial Statement also disclosed expenditure of $160 per week for (unspecified) other adults. It was also undermined by evidence he gave under cross-examination as to substantial funds passing between them for the purpose of payment of significant expenses incurred by each other, though he sought to classify such transactions as loans.
The financial support said to be provided to Ms O by the husband was also difficult to understand in light of his evidence that Ms O had been in a position to lend him substantial funds for the purpose of payment of his legal fees.

(n) the terms of any order made or proposed to be made under section 79 in relation to (i) the property of the parties or (ii) vested bankruptcy property in relation to a bankrupt party

In the present case, there are final orders in force pursuant to section 79 with respect to the property of the parties. I have regard to the terms of those orders, and particularly to the fact that the mortgage liability which constitutes the wife’s major liability and expense was retained by her pursuant to those orders. I have regard also to the fact that the wife has before the Court an as yet undetermined application to set those orders aside based (inter alia) on an asserted miscarriage of justice. These issues are discussed in greater detail later in these reasons.

(na) any child support under the Child Support (Assessment) Act 1989 that a party to the marriage has provided, is to provide, or might be liable to provide in the future, for a child of the marriage

The husband pays child support to the wife for X in the sum of $311 per week.

DISCUSSION

Can the wife support herself adequately?

I accept the wife’s evidence with respect to her income, as outlined above, in relation to which she was not challenged. When considering the wife’s income, I include the weekly sum of $325, being her half-share of the rental income for the Suburb H property, which did not appear in her Financial Statement.
For the purposes of considering the wife’s ability to provide for her own support, I disregard the income tested benefit received by her, as I am required to do, and do not include the child support payments she receives from the husband, which are for the purpose of support of X and not the wife.
I therefore assess the wife’s income for the purposes of the present application as $1,811 per week.
The monthly principal and interest repayments towards the wife’s mortgage loan are $5,501 per month or $1,269 per week. This is the most significant of the wife’s expenses and these repayments alone exceed her after-tax employment income. These repayments were suspended on the basis of hardship between December 2024 and May 2025. There is no evidence before the Court as to whether the payments could be converted to interest-only payments pending the determination of these proceedings, or, if so, the amount by which this would reduce the required monthly repayments.
Many of the wife’s expenses are non-discretionary in nature and I consider that most of her expenditure is reasonable. She deposed to spending approximately $977 per week on income tax, insurances, rates (which are in arrears), and motor vehicle registration. It was put to her in cross-examination that her expenditure on life insurance ($128 per week) was excessive. She explained that it was important to her to ensure that her debts would be paid so that the parties’ children would be secure in the event that anything happened to her. I accept that this is reasonable.
The expenses outlined at Part N of the wife’s Financial Statement total $1,793 per week, of which $519 is allocated as being for herself and $1,274 per week is for the children’s expenses. Although the figures actually included in the “for you” column in Part N of the wife’s Financial Statement total $514 rather than $519, this appears to be a result of the erroneous exclusion of the figure of $5 which appears for “repairs – furnishings and appliances” in the “total” column, but has been omitted from the “for you” column. I accept that $519 is the correct figure when that error is accounted for.
As has been outlined in a number of authorities, the reasonable needs of an applicant for spousal maintenance are to be assessed according to the amount they require for their own support, and not what they require for support of children.[36] That is, a spousal maintenance application cannot be used as a “back door” application for, or as a substitute for, a child support departure order or an adult child maintenance order.
To the extent that the listed expenses relate to the wife herself, and are not expenses for the children, I am satisfied that they are reasonable.
The wife’s evidence was of having received significant support from third parties to enable her to meet her expenses, including the provision of fruit, vegetables and other food items from each of her parents (recorded in her Financial Statement as being $100 per week from her mother and $25 per week from her father), her mother cooking meals for her and the children, her mother lending her money and paying expenses on her behalf, the parties’ daughter Ms B lending her money, and friends and the church pastor and parishioners all lending her money.
The husband gave unchallenged evidence that the wife had requested spousal maintenance from him prior to the commencement of the proceedings but had not raised it again between 28 June 2024, at which time he indicated that he did not have capacity to pay it, and 29 November 2024, at which time she filed an Initiating Application which contained an interim application for spousal maintenance.
The husband further deposed that following the filing of that application, the wife did not press her spousal maintenance application at any of the hearings before the Court prior to the filling of the present application on 15 September 2025. Although I do not know what the wife sought at such hearings in terms of procedural orders and whether she pressed that her maintenance application be listed, I note that there were no interim hearings held between the commencement of the proceedings and the filling of the current application, and there was therefore no hearing at which the wife could have pressed the application itself.
I do not understand there to be a serious dispute that the wife is unable to meet her present expenses including the mortgage liability from her income alone. The husband agreed that he had made substantial contributions to the wife’s expenses including the mortgage during the disputed reconciliation period. It was not suggested that she had demonstrated a capacity to do so unaided.
The home loan repayment pause between December 2024 to May 2025 at least partly explains the wife’s ability to support herself during that period without spousal maintenance. The wife also gave unchallenged evidence of having redrawn on the mortgage (thus eroding the equity in the former matrimonial home) to meet her expenses during 2024.
In these circumstances, I do not accept (if indeed it is suggested by the husband) that the wife’s failure to press her spousal maintenance application at an earlier time in the proceedings is indicative of an ability on her part to provide adequately for her own support.
The wife accepted that if the Suburb F block were sold, she could discharge the mortgage and thus significantly reduce her expenses. This would also reduce the wife’s liability to pay rates. Her Financial Statement indicates that the rates for that property are in the sum of $7,318 per annum. The wife accepted that to the extent she was in exigent circumstances, it was almost wholly as a consequence of the mortgage repayments. She is adamantly opposed to selling the block and does not consider that she should be required to do so in order to repay the mortgage in circumstances in which the liability in question was derived from expenditure incurred by the husband.
Similarly, realisation of the wife’s interest in the Suburb H property would likely yield sufficient funds to enable her to discharge her mortgage liability and thus reduce her necessary expenses substantially. The wife gave evidence that her co-owner, being her brother, is opposed to its sale and is unable to buy out her interest in the property.
Counsel for the husband submitted that any inability on the part of the wife to provide adequately for her own support was wholly caused by her own unreasonable refusal to sell either of the real properties in which she holds substantial interests. As such, it was submitted, the wife’s predicament was not, properly characterised, an inability to meet her own needs at all, but rather a refusal to do so combined with a desire that the husband meet those needs instead. It was also submitted that the wife’s steadfast refusal to consider selling real estate in order to alleviate her needs was indicative of a lack of reasonable judgment on her behalf.
The wife relied on the Full Court’s decision in Mitchell & Mitchell (“Mitchell”),[37] in which their Honours held that “[t]he days are long gone when it is necessary for an applicant for maintenance to use up all of her assets and capital in order to satisfy the requirement that she is unable to support herself ‘adequately.’” Similarly, in Bevan & Bevan (“Bevan”),[38] the Full Court said that:
... we do not think that the law requires that a wife should deplete an already comparatively meagre capital sum, to enable a much higher earning husband to avoid his obligation to maintain a former spouse who is in financial circumstances such as those in which she finds herself. ...
Counsel for the wife emphasised that in Mitchell (as here), final property orders had been made, and submitted that although the parties’ asset positions were a relevant consideration, an expectation that a party should be required to use their assets to meet their expenses was not something the Court should accept.
Counsel for the husband submitted that Mitchell could be distinguished on the basis that the capital in question in that case had been funds which were required by the wife to provide herself with a home, whereas in this case, the proposal was for the sale of a vacant block of land or another property in which the wife does not reside, and there was no suggestion that the wife would be unable to retain her longstanding place of residence. The same can be said of Bevan.
I note that their Honours in Mitchell went on to say that “[w]here the line is to be drawn will depend upon the circumstances of individual cases.” Plainly, these authorities do not support the proposition that an applicant for spousal maintenance is entitled to retain capital assets of limitless value without the circumstance of such ownership affecting the assessment of whether they can support themselves adequately.[39]
Were I being asked to determine an application for a final order for periodic spousal maintenance in circumstances in which the final orders made under section 79 were to endure, the argument that the wife could not be said to lack the capacity to provide for her own support by virtue of the fact that she could sell one or both of the real properties in which she has an interest and in which she does not reside, thereby discharging her mortgage liability and substantially reducing her expenses, would have considerable force.
Furthermore, I accept that the circumstances of the present application are different from the more usual scenario in which interim orders for spousal maintenance are sought, which is pending the finalisation of property settlement proceedings pursuant to section 79. In the present case, there are final property orders in place. Until such time (if any) as the wife satisfies the onus borne by her with respect to establishment of one or more of the grounds set out in section 79A of the Act and persuades the Court to exercise its discretion in favour of varying or setting aside the final orders, those orders will remain in place. It certainly cannot be assumed that those orders will be disturbed or that there will ultimately be any further or other order made in the wife’s favour pursuant to section 79.
The fact of, and circumstances surrounding, the wife’s pending section 79A application are, however, of significance to the question of whether the wife should be expected to divest herself of capital holdings before it can be said that she is unable to support herself adequately on an interim basis.
The wife has owned the Suburb F block since prior to the parties’ marriage. She gave evidence that in 2004, she had repaid the mortgage she had originally taken when the block was purchased and had at that time owned it unencumbered, but that the block had subsequently been encumbered by a further mortgage securing liabilities associated with the husband’s business dealings prior to the parties’ first separation. That liability was subsequently refinanced as part of the implementation of the final orders in 2016.
The wife’s interest in the Suburb H property was inherited by her. The evidence before the court suggests that the husband made no contribution to the acquisition of either property, and does not establish any contribution of significance made by him to their maintenance or improvement.
The wife alleges in the context of the section 79A proceedings that the husband misled her and the Court with respect to the true nature of various liabilities and with respect to the use to which he had put funds generated by him prior to the parties’ 2013 separation. She alleges that had proper disclosure been made by him, the outcome of the original proceedings would have been significantly better for her. She resists being required to sell assets in order to meet her living expenses in circumstances where she has taken action to rectify what she asserts is a miscarriage of justice.
The wife is aggrieved by the fact that (at least as understood by her) the mortgage for which she bears responsibility was derived from the husband’s business and other dealings. Significantly, she alleges that this includes dealings which formed part of the suppression of evidence giving rise to the asserted miscarriage of justice, in circumstances in which she asserts that the indebtedness with which she was left arose (directly or indirectly) as a consequence of the dealings undertaken by the husband which were not disclosed by him and from which he later benefited to her exclusion. On the wife’s case, these are liabilities the responsibility for which ought not have been visited upon her, and she asserts that this would not have occurred but for the suppression of evidence on the part of the husband at the time of making the final orders.
Ordinarily, in circumstances in which she had accepted responsibility for the liability in question pursuant to final orders made under section 79 and as part of an overall settlement accepted by the court which made the orders as having been just and equitable, neither the purpose for which the liability had originally arisen nor the wife’s feelings about having ended up responsible for its repayment, would be of any moment. Similarly, reluctance on the part of the wife to insist on the sale of the Suburb H property over the objection of its co-owner would not ordinarily justify refusal to realise that interest in order to meet her needs.
In this case, however, the wife alleges that a miscarriage of justice has arisen as a result of a suppression of evidence on the part of the husband such that had there been full and proper disclosure, she would not have been left with responsibility for the liability in question. That is, her responsibility for the very liability which she is unable to meet, and which the husband argues she should liquidate real estate holdings in order to meet, is (or is part of) the miscarriage of justice she alleges.
Although I am not presently in a position to form a view as to the likely success of the wife’s section 79A application, the evidence presently before the Court (albeit untested) does not appear to suggest that it is hopeless, and the Court is not being asked to dismiss it summarily.
If the wife does ultimately succeed in establishing the miscarriage of justice she asserts, an outcome of the present application which left her with no alternative but to take the irreversible step of selling real property she wishes to retain would contribute substantially to the miscarriage of justice visited upon the wife.
I do not consider it proper that the Court make orders which would have the effect of requiring the wife to divest herself of capital assets which have long been held by her, were acquired by her with minimal contribution by the husband, and can be expected to provide an investment for her future, in order to service a liability which she alleges was, and which may have been, foisted upon her as a consequence of a miscarriage of justice, so that she can meet her needs for the relatively short period of time pending determination of her application pursuant to section 79A.
Furthermore, it is an agreed fact that the view of the husband, who has substantial experience in property development, that with the investment of approximately $100,000 to $200,000 in improvements, the value of the Suburb H property would likely be doubled. The wife is not presently in a position to make such an investment, but it is plausible that if she establishes her claim with respect to the asserted miscarriage of justice, this would change. She gave evidence that her brother is similarly not presently in a position to make the required investment, and that if the property were to be sold, it would need to be sold “as is.”
Requiring the wife to sell or dispose of her interest in this property in its present unimproved state prior to the determination of her section 79A application could therefore result in a substantial lost opportunity for her, further compounding any miscarriage of justice that is ultimately established. There is also a risk that any such potential miscarriage of justice may be compounded by the impact, both financial and emotional, of being required against her wishes to use legal processes to force the sale of the Suburb H property against her brother’s will.
There is no evidence before the Court as to the likely taxation consequences of either of the proposed sales or the extent to which any such consequences would or could render the sale of one of the properties alone insufficient to extinguish the wife’s mortgage liability.
I also have regard to the realities of the likely timeframe involved with a sale or realisation of the wife’s interest in either of the real properties in question. In relation to the Suburb F block, the property would need to be listed for sale, marketed, sold, and then a settlement period, presently of unknown duration, would follow, before any funds would be received by the wife.
The same is true of with respect to the Suburb H property, save that in the absence of consent of the wife’s brother, the sale process could not even begin prior to the wife taking legal proceedings of unknown duration (and involving unknown expense) against her brother.
In those circumstances, whilst the realisation of her real property holdings may provide a long-term solution to the wife’s circumstances of financial need, I do not accept that this solution is realistically available to meet her immediate need for interim support pending the resolution of the extant proceedings, which are listed for trial later this year.
For all of these reasons, I am satisfied on the balance of probabilities that the wife has met the threshold requirement of establishing that she is unable to support herself adequately “for any other adequate reason” (that reason being the combined effect of her reasonable expenses exceeding her available income and the Court’s view that it is not proper to require her to divest herself of capital assets at this time) pending the final determination of these proceedings.

If not, what are the wife’s reasonable needs?

As I have discussed, Part N of the wife’s Financial Statement records that of her weekly expenses, a total of $1,274 is for the children. I refer again to the authorities cautioning against the use of spousal maintenance orders for the purpose of meeting children’s expenses.
Counsel for the wife submitted, and counsel for the husband accepted, as do I, that there is some overlap in the provision of household expenses for the wife and for the other occupants of her household, being the children. This issue has also been considered in a number of authorities. For example, in Redman & Redman,[40] the Full Court said as follows:
...a strict line between costs referable to the custodial parent and those referable to the children cannot always be drawn with clarity. Some expenditure relating to the household as a whole, such as the provision of housing, electricity, fuel, transport, and possibly food and groceries, cannot be strictly divided. Where an application is made which covers both the custodial parent and the children those expenses can with some justification be allocated under either heading. ...
In Padnall & Padnall,[41] Strickland J (sitting in the appellate jurisdiction) held that it was necessary to “carefully consider what (if any) costs of support of the children should be taken into account” in assessing the needs of the needs of the applicant for maintenance.
I do not consider that the relevant authorities, when considered collectively, impose a blanket prohibition on the inclusion of any expenses relating to the children whatsoever when assessing the needs of an applicant in the context of a spousal maintenance application, but rather require caution in doing so.
Plainly, X’s expenses must be disregarded to the extent that they are covered by the $311 per week in child support which is paid by the husband. Unfortunately, the wife failed to distinguish in Part N of her Financial Statement between X as a child (by using the “for children” column) and Ms B as an adult (by using the “other adults”) column despite the form requiring the expenses to be recorded in such a fashion. This has made the exercise tracing the application of the child support payments unnecessary difficult.
Many of the expenses recorded at Part N of the wife’s Financial Statement are not areas of “overlap” but expenses purely related to the children, which I consider are expenses which would properly form the part of a child support departure application or adult child maintenance application and not a spousal maintenance application.
I also accept the submission made on behalf of the husband that some of these expenses appear excessive, particularly those relating to services such as physiotherapy, which includes massages, and indeed the wife herself appeared uncertain as to whether these expenses had accurately been recorded when questioned in the witness box, agreeing that the figure appearing in her Financial Statement seemed high.
Doing the best I can with the unavoidable lack of precision arising from overlapping expenses, I will include the full amounts claimed by the wife for those expenses which relate to the running of the household as a whole and which cannot easily be attributed to individual household members or be expected to fluctuate substantially based on their presence or absence, being household supplies, house repairs, gas, electricity and internet (a total of $188 per week).
Although the manner in which the wife’s Financial Statement was completed precludes precise calculations in this regard, I am satisfied that in adopting this approach I have avoided impermissibly double counting expenditure with respect to X which is covered by the child support paid by the husband.
I will otherwise include only the expenses listed at Part N of the wife’s Financial Statement which are attributable to the wife personally and not the children (a total of $389).
This brings the wife’s total reasonable expenditure on her own self-support as outlined in Part N of her Financial Statement to $577 per week. When combined with the balance of her expenditure as outlined elsewhere in that document, the total comes to $2,823. I accept that the wife may have some additional expenditure associated with the Suburb H property which may be being met from the rental income derived from that property, but her failure to adduce evidence of any such expenditure precludes me from taking it into account.
Having regard to these considerations, I assess the wife’s reasonable needs to be $1,012 per week, being her reasonable expenditure of $2,823 per week required for her own self-support, less her income of $1,811 per week.

What capacity does the respondent have to meet those needs?

I have already discussed the evidence before the Court as to the husband’s income and his expenditure on legal fees.
The wife deposed to the husband having engaged in substantial discretionary expenditure in the months preceding the filing of the present application, as demonstrated in his bank statements for that period. This expenditure included $1,334 on clothing and luxury items, $1,182 on takeaway and restaurants in a two month period from one account and $3,833 in a six month period from another, and $303 on gambling. The wife further deposed to there having been $11,200 in unexplained transfers to unknown accounts and $4,200 in ATM withdrawals across a six month period from one account.
The wife further alleged (without reference to specific dates) that the husband’s bank account statements revealed substantial discretionary expenditure including a purchase of jewellery for $7,312, the purchase of two cars including the $36,795 Motor Vehicle 1 already referred to, discharge of Ms O’s motor vehicle loan in the total sum of $67,506 (as already mentioned), payment of Ms O’s legal fees in the sum of $52,679 (as already mentioned), unexplained cash and EFT withdrawals and deposits totalling tens of thousands of dollars, and payment for renovation works being undertaken on real properties owned by him.
It was submitted on behalf of the wife, and I accept, that the husband’s asserted inability to meet his own expenses (as reflected by the weekly deficit recorded in his Financial Statement) was contradicted by the evidence of his expenditure, and that his financial conduct, particularly his discretionary expenditure, was consistent with a capacity to meet the wife’s reasonable needs.
The wife alleged in her affidavit that the husband had overstated his loan repayments by a total of $721 per week (calculated by reference to the monthly repayments made without taking into account unknown information as to whether such payments exceeded the required monthly payments). This would have the effect of converting the weekly shortfall of $128 asserted in the husband’s Financial Statement to a surplus of $593. However, in the witness box, the wife accepted that the figures given by the husband were the amounts that he actually paid. She clarified that her challenge was to the suggestion that he was required to pay those amounts but admitted that this was based on speculation. The wife further deposed to the fact that the payments relate to investment properties and are therefore tax deductible, but this does not change the required weekly outlay and would be reflected separately in the assessment of tax payable by the husband.
As outlined earlier, the husband deposed to having various non-mortgage liabilities. These included:
(a) A debt of $18,000 owed to the ATO. The wife gave unchallenged evidence that no disclosure had been provided to demonstrate either the existence of this asserted debt or the circumstances in which it had arisen.

(b) A debt of $103,235.66 to his self-managed superannuation fund relating to withdrawals from the accounts of the fund (for purposes which were not explained). The wife gave unchallenged evidence that the husband’s bank account statements revealed that part of these funds had been spent on personal expenses such as travel, entertainment, restaurant meals and gambling.

(c) A debt of $16,710 to Y School on account of school fees for X (being his share of a debt owed jointly with the wife). It is an agreed fact that the husband had previously been meeting the school fees to the extent that they had been being paid.

(d) The asserted debts associated with payment of his legal fees, as already discussed.

The evidence before the Court does not establish that the husband is in fact making any repayments towards any of these asserted liabilities. Nor any is there any evidence to suggest that there is any urgency whatsoever with respect to the debt owed to the superannuation fund in particular. The husband’s evidence was that the agreement reached with respect to the asserted loans for legal fees was simply for repayment to be made at a later date. He gave no evidence of any required interim repayments.
The wife pointed to expenditure on the husband’s part of $632.45 per month by way of direct debit to the husband’s accountants, and alleged that no explanation for this expenditure had been provided in circumstances in which the husband’s evidence was that he was not operating any business entities.
The husband acknowledged that during the earlier proceedings between the parties, he had ceased making contributions to the mortgage and other expenses and had asserted that he lacked the capacity to do so, before subsequently resuming those payments in the context of the parties undertaking what he characterises as an attempt to reconcile. He accepted that his withdrawal of financial support was “like a repeat” of the earlier period.
Counsel for the wife urged me to make adverse credit findings with respect to the husband, and particularly to find that the ledger was a fabrication. In my view, it is not necessary to do so. The evidence given by and adduced by the husband was so contradictory and confused as to be unable to be relied upon, irrespective of whether it was deliberately misleading or untruthful.
Counsel for the wife urged the Court to draw an inference that the husband has significantly greater income than that which he has disclosed, and particularly that he has sufficient income to generate the sum of $99,000 across a period of approximately 90 days.
It was submitted on behalf of the husband that the Court could draw an inference that he had a greater income than that demonstrated in his Financial Statement only if satisfied that there was no other explanation for the circumstance that the costs notices filed on his behalf (which I note have been acknowledged by him to have been filed in accordance with his instructions) expressly stated that his expenditure on legal fees had been derived from his income. It was suggested that there might be “all kinds of reasons” why a litigant would characterise funds provided by third parties as their own income and that it was, for example, “conceivable” that the husband had “made a mistake.”
The difficulty with this submission is that there is no evidence whatsoever of any other explanation for this circumstance, whether mistake or otherwise, rendering the suggestion mere speculation. As the statement of agreed facts makes clear, the husband caused the Court to be advised that he had raised the funds in question from his income. It is for him, and not for the wife, to establish why he did so in circumstances in which he now says that was not accurate.
The husband was on notice of the significance of the characterisation in his costs notices of the source of the funds applied to his legal fees from the time of filing of the wife’s affidavit on 15 September 2025, over four months prior to the hearing. He replied to the relevant passage of that affidavit in his own subsequent affidavit, but said only that he had not advised his solicitors that he needed to borrow funds in order to pay his legal fees. He filed no affidavit from any of the persons said to have lent him money and adduced no documentary evidence to support his assertions, such as the relevant bank statements. He was cross-examined about the issue and proffered no explanation.
If the husband sought to have the Court accept that there is some other explanation for the characterisation of the funds applied to his legal fees as income as recorded in his costs notices in accordance with his instructions other than that information being accurate, it was up to him to put evidence supporting such an explanation before the Court. He did not do so.
Although the confusing and contradictory nature of the evidence before the Court precludes precise findings, the evidence enables me to draw the following inferences and conclusions with respect to the husband’s financial circumstances:
(a) The husband has had the means at his disposal to generate in excess of $200,000 for the purposes of funding his legal expenses in the period between the commencement of the proceedings on 29 November 2024 and the filing of his most recent costs notice on 15 January 2026 (a period of less than 14 months);

(b) Over $99,000 of that sum was able to be generated by him within a period of only approximately 90 days;

(c) The funds applied by the husband to his legal fees prior to October 2025, which totalled in excess of $140,000, were repeatedly contemporaneously characterised by him as having been derived from his income;

(d) No independent documentary or affidavit evidence has been produced by husband, being the party with the power to produce such evidence, to support his assertions to the contrary;

(e) The husband has substantial funds available to him to meet discretionary expenses including lifestyle expenses and substantial payments to meet the needs of third parties;

(f) The wife’s needs as I have assessed are modest by comparison to the husband’s discretionary and other expenditure; and

(g) The husband’s access to and ability to raise funds substantially exceeds that of the wife.

Although precise findings as to the husband’s income are precluded by the state of the evidence before the Court, this is a consequence of actions taken by the husband including but not limited to the manner in which he has presented his case and the conflicting information he has provided to his solicitors for the purpose of preparation of the various documents before the Court. In such circumstances, it is not proper that he be the party to benefit from the resulting uncertainty.
Having regard to these considerations, I am satisfied on the balance of probabilities that the husband has the capacity to meet the wife’s needs as I have assessed them.

What order is proper?

In all the circumstances, I find that it is proper to order that the husband pay spousal maintenance to the wife in the sum of $1,012 per week.

CONCLUSION

The Court was advised that the parties had agreed that any question of costs arising from the present application would be reserved to the trial and the orders will so provide.
For all of the reasons outlined above, I otherwise make the orders as set out at the commencement of these reasons.
